bacteria.name: Bacteria
bacteria.description: Harmful bacteria like E. coli and Salmonella cause severe gastrointestinal illness.
bacteria.sources: Open defecation, sewage overflow, animal waste near water sources, and untreated groundwater are the main entry points. Flooding dramatically increases bacterial contamination in surface water.
bacteria.healthRisks: Causes severe gastrointestinal illness — diarrhea, vomiting, cramps, and fever. Can be fatal for young children, elderly people, and those with weakened immune systems. E. coli O157:H7 can cause kidney failure.
bacteria.detection: Field test strips can detect coliform bacteria within minutes. Laboratory culture analysis confirms specific species. Assume contamination in any untreated surface water or after flooding.

viruses.name: Viruses
viruses.description: Waterborne viruses including Hepatitis A and Norovirus spread through contaminated water.
viruses.sources: Human sewage contamination is the primary source. Flooding, inadequate sanitation near wells, and infected individuals using shared water sources spread viral contamination rapidly.
viruses.healthRisks: Hepatitis A causes liver inflammation and jaundice lasting weeks. Norovirus and Rotavirus cause acute vomiting and diarrhea leading to dangerous dehydration, especially in children under five.
viruses.detection: Standard field test strips do not detect viruses. Specialized laboratory PCR or immunoassay testing is required. Treat all untreated surface water as potentially contaminated — viruses survive longer than bacteria.

protozoa.name: Protozoa
protozoa.description: Parasites like Giardia and Cryptosporidium are resistant to chlorine and cause diarrhoea.
protozoa.sources: Animal and human feces contaminating surface water, livestock grazing near water sources, and irrigation runoff. Cysts survive for months in cold water and resist standard chlorine disinfection doses.
protozoa.healthRisks: Giardia and Cryptosporidium cause prolonged diarrhea, cramps, and dehydration lasting weeks. Cryptosporidiosis can be life-threatening for immunocompromised individuals and young children.
protozoa.detection: Microscopic examination or immunofluorescence testing by a laboratory. Field tests are unreliable. Assume protozoan contamination in any untreated river, lake, or pond water.

turbidity.name: Turbidity / Sediment
turbidity.description: Suspended particles that make water cloudy and can harbour pathogens.
turbidity.sources: Soil erosion and runoff during rain, disturbed sediment from flooding, algae blooms, and construction near water bodies. Seasonally elevated in rivers after heavy rainfall.
turbidity.healthRisks: Suspended particles shelter and shield pathogens from disinfection, making turbid water harder to treat safely. High turbidity is a strong indicator of other contamination and makes water unsafe to drink untreated.
turbidity.detection: Visually apparent when severe — cloudy or brown water. Turbidity meters measure in NTU units. The WHO guideline is under 1 NTU for treated water. Turbid water should never be considered safe without treatment.

heavy_metals.name: Heavy Metals
heavy_metals.description: Lead, arsenic, and mercury cause serious long-term health effects.
heavy_metals.sources: Corroding lead pipes in older plumbing, industrial discharge and mining runoff, agricultural pesticides, and natural geological deposits rich in arsenic, cadmium, or chromium.
heavy_metals.healthRisks: Lead causes irreversible neurological damage and developmental delays in children — there is no safe exposure level. Arsenic is a Group 1 carcinogen linked to bladder and lung cancer. Mercury damages kidneys and the nervous system over time.
heavy_metals.detection: Laboratory analysis or certified home test kits are required for accurate results. ICP-MS laboratory testing is the gold standard. Test strips for lead exist but have limited sensitivity. Water from buildings with old pipes should always be tested.

chlorine.name: Chlorine / Taste & Odour
chlorine.description: Residual chlorine from municipal treatment causes unpleasant taste and odour.
chlorine.sources: Intentionally added by municipal water treatment as a disinfectant. Levels are regulated but may be elevated after treatment plant interventions. Rarely present in groundwater unless industrial contamination has occurred.
chlorine.healthRisks: Residual chlorine causes unpleasant taste and odour. Chlorine reacts with organic matter to form trihalomethanes (THMs) — disinfection by-products linked to increased bladder cancer risk with decades of exposure.
chlorine.detection: Inexpensive DPD test strips and drop kits are widely available at pool and aquarium supply stores. Detectable by smell at concentrations above 0.3 mg/L. Municipal water quality reports disclose chlorine levels.

nitrates.name: Nitrates
nitrates.description: High nitrate levels from agriculture are dangerous especially for infants.
nitrates.sources: Agricultural fertilizer runoff is the dominant source. Septic system leakage, animal feedlots, and natural soil microbial decomposition also contribute. Highest risk in areas with intensive farming near water sources.
nitrates.healthRisks: Causes methemoglobinemia in infants under 6 months — nitrates prevent blood from carrying oxygen, causing bluish skin. This 'blue baby syndrome' can be rapidly fatal. Long-term adult exposure may increase colorectal cancer risk.
nitrates.detection: Nitrate test strips are widely available at aquarium and pool supply stores. Laboratory analysis is required for accurate regulatory compliance. The WHO limit is 50 mg/L for adults, but 10 mg/L for infant safety.

fluoride.name: Fluoride
fluoride.description: Excess fluoride causes dental and skeletal fluorosis.
fluoride.sources: Natural volcanic rock formations and sedimentary deposits leach fluoride into groundwater — a major issue in East Africa, South Asia, and parts of China. Industrial waste from fertiliser and aluminium production also contaminates water.
fluoride.healthRisks: At levels above 1.5 mg/L (WHO guideline), fluoride causes dental fluorosis — mottled and weakened teeth. At levels above 4 mg/L, skeletal fluorosis develops, causing joint pain, bone damage, and in severe cases crippling deformity.
fluoride.detection: Requires laboratory analysis or specialized SPADNS photometric test kits. Standard field test strips have limited accuracy for fluoride. Geological maps of endemic fluoride areas provide useful preliminary information.

boiling.name: Boiling
boiling.description: The most accessible disinfection method — kills bacteria, viruses, and protozoa.
boiling.howItWorks: Sustained boiling at 100 °C kills all biological pathogens by denaturing proteins and disrupting cell membranes. One minute of rolling boil is sufficient at sea level; three minutes above 2000 m altitude.
boiling.limitations: Does not remove chemical contaminants, heavy metals, nitrates, or fluoride. Requires a reliable fuel source and adds cost and smoke exposure. Re-contamination is possible if water is stored in a dirty container.
boiling.typicalUse: Emergency disinfection in any setting, daily household use where fuel is accessible, and as a last-resort backup when other systems fail.

biosand.name: Biosand Filter
biosand.description: A DIY sand filter with a biological layer that removes pathogens and turbidity.
biosand.howItWorks: Water percolates slowly through layers of fine sand and gravel. Over 2–3 weeks, a biological layer (Schmutzdecke) forms on the surface and consumes incoming pathogens. Physical straining simultaneously removes turbidity.
biosand.limitations: Requires 1–3 weeks to establish the biological layer. Slow flow rate (0.3–0.6 L/min). Ineffective against dissolved chemicals, viruses (partial only), and taste or odour. The bio-layer dies if the filter dries out.
biosand.typicalUse: Rural and peri-urban communities with consistently turbid surface water where local construction materials are available and initial setup can be supervised.

ceramic_filtration.name: Ceramic Filtration
ceramic_filtration.description: Porous ceramic pots remove bacteria, protozoa, and suspended solids.
ceramic_filtration.howItWorks: Micropores in fired clay (0.1–1 µm) physically block bacteria and protozoa. Many pots are impregnated with colloidal silver for additional bactericidal effect. Water flows by gravity into a lower storage vessel.
ceramic_filtration.limitations: Slow throughput (1–3 L/hr). Fragile — cracks eliminate filtration. Requires regular cleaning to prevent clogging. No effect on viruses, dissolved chemicals, or nitrates. Storage vessel must be kept clean and covered.
ceramic_filtration.typicalUse: Household point-of-use treatment where the main concern is bacterial contamination and turbidity. Widely deployed in sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and Central America.

activated_carbon.name: Activated Carbon
activated_carbon.description: Adsorbs chlorine, pesticides, and heavy metals through a porous carbon block.
activated_carbon.howItWorks: Highly porous activated carbon adsorbs organic molecules, chlorine by-products, pesticides, and some heavy metals onto its vast internal surface area (up to 1500 m²/g). Water passes through a solid carbon block or granular bed under gravity or pressure.
activated_carbon.limitations: Does not kill or remove biological pathogens alone — must be combined with disinfection. The carbon bed saturates and must be replaced every 3–6 months. Ineffective against nitrates, fluoride, and dissolved salts.
activated_carbon.typicalUse: Polishing stage after disinfection — improving taste, odour, and colour of chlorinated tap water, or reducing chemical load before other treatment stages.

uv_disinfection.name: UV Disinfection
uv_disinfection.description: Ultraviolet light destroys the DNA of bacteria, viruses, and protozoa.
uv_disinfection.howItWorks: UV-C light at 254 nm penetrates microbial cells and disrupts DNA/RNA, preventing reproduction. A UV lamp in a flow-through chamber irradiates passing water. Effective against bacteria, viruses, and protozoa including Cryptosporidium.
uv_disinfection.limitations: No effect on turbid water — suspended particles shield microbes from UV. Provides no residual protection after treatment. Requires stable electricity and annual lamp replacement. Does not remove chemical contaminants.
uv_disinfection.typicalUse: Final disinfection stage for water that has already been pre-filtered for turbidity. Used in household under-sink systems, community kiosks, and field water treatment trains.

reverse_osmosis.name: Reverse Osmosis
reverse_osmosis.description: A semi-permeable membrane removes virtually all dissolved contaminants.
reverse_osmosis.howItWorks: Pressurised water is forced through a membrane with 0.0001 µm pores, rejecting dissolved salts, heavy metals, nitrates, fluoride, and microorganisms. Rejected contaminants are flushed away as brine; purified water is collected separately.
reverse_osmosis.limitations: Wastes 3–4 litres per litre produced. Requires inlet pressure of 4–8 bar — usually needs a booster pump. Expensive to install and maintain. Removes beneficial minerals. Membranes must be replaced every 2–3 years.
reverse_osmosis.typicalUse: Households or small businesses facing dissolved contamination that cannot be addressed otherwise — high salinity, nitrates, fluoride, or heavy metals — with access to reliable electricity.

chlorination.name: Chlorination
chlorination.description: Adding chlorine tablets or solution disinfects water and provides residual protection.
chlorination.howItWorks: Chlorine reacts with water to form hypochlorous acid, which penetrates microbial cell walls and disrupts vital enzymes, killing bacteria and viruses within 30 minutes. A residual of 0.2–0.5 mg/L maintains protection during storage.
chlorination.limitations: Protozoa including Cryptosporidium and Giardia are highly resistant. Reacts with organic matter to form potentially carcinogenic trihalomethanes. No effect on chemical contaminants, heavy metals, or turbidity. Taste and odour can be unpleasant.
chlorination.typicalUse: Point-of-use disinfection using tablets (NaDCC) or household bleach. Especially effective in emergencies and displacement settings where portability and low cost are critical.

sediment_5um.name: Sediment Filter 5µm
sediment_5um.description: Removes sand, rust, and suspended particles down to 5 microns.

activated_carbon_block.name: Activated Carbon Block
activated_carbon_block.description: Removes chlorine, taste, odour, and some heavy metals.

ceramic_candle.name: Ceramic Candle Filter
ceramic_candle.description: A porous ceramic element that blocks bacteria and protozoa.

biosand_diy.name: DIY Biosand Filter
biosand_diy.description: A locally built sand filter with biological filtration layer.

uv_lamp_6w.name: UV Lamp 6W
uv_lamp_6w.description: A 6-watt UV lamp for disinfecting up to 6 litres per minute.

ro_membrane_75gpd.name: RO Membrane 75 GPD
ro_membrane_75gpd.description: Reverse osmosis membrane removing dissolved salts and contaminants.

slow_sand_diy.name: DIY Slow Sand Filter
slow_sand_diy.description: A large-scale gravity filter using fine sand to remove pathogens.

chlorine_tablet.name: Chlorine Tablets (NaDCC)
chlorine_tablet.description: Sodium dichloroisocyanurate tablets for point-of-use disinfection.

booster_pump.name: Booster Pump
booster_pump.description: Increases inlet water pressure to meet the requirements of high-pressure filters such as reverse osmosis.
